---
title: "Session 15 · Course Synthesis: SLR → MLR → GLM"
---

## Overview

This wrap-up session has no new reading or lab --- instead, it invites you to look back across the whole arc. We began with the *t*-test and simple linear regression, saw a single dummy reproduce that *t*-test, watched several dummies become ANOVA, added continuous predictors for multiple regression, let effects bend through interactions, learned to trust models only after diagnostics, crossed to binary outcomes with logistic regression, and finally saw every one of these as a special case of the Generalized Linear Model. The goal now is to consolidate that journey: to see one family where you once saw many tools, and to choose the right member of it for a research question of your own.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Trace the conceptual arc from the *t*-test and simple regression through to the GLM.
- Connect each milestone model to the ones before and after it as special or general cases.
- Choose an appropriate model for a given research question and outcome type.
- Reflect on the assumptions and diagnostics that decide whether a chosen model is trustworthy.

## This session's materials

- {{< fa clipboard-list >}} **Project:** [Project 2 — Final Report due](/assignment/project-2-final.html)

For a guided review of any milestone below, revisit the [Lecture Notes](/notes/){target="_blank"}.

## Key concepts

| Milestone | What it added to the arc |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Simple linear regression** | Modeling an outcome as a straight-line function of one continuous predictor. |
| **Dummy = *t*-test** | A single 0/1 predictor reproduces the two-sample *t*-test exactly. |
| **ANOVA** | Several dummies compare three or more group means within the regression framework. |
| **Multiple regression** | Continuous and categorical predictors together, each interpreted holding the others fixed. |
| **Interactions** | Letting one predictor's effect depend on the value of another. |
| **Diagnostics** | Checking residuals, influence, and assumptions to judge whether a fitted model can be trusted. |
| **Logistic regression** | Extending the framework to binary outcomes via the log-odds link. |
| **Generalized Linear Model** | The unifying family from which every earlier model emerges as a special case. |
